And again, Eili, I love when we do these calls together. Feel free to jump in and add all your older value you have to give. We'll do fantastic. So I prepared two things for today, which I like to give you a little deep dive on. And the first is about the principle of social proof. And my story is mainly about why is social proof so powerful that a lot of, not a lot of people go into discussions. Yeah, but I always go against the crowd or I would not do that. Or I see this social proof now all over the internet. Think about booking.com. Does this really work? There are more and more comments going on like this or I get a hold of them or people ask me those questions. And I think booking.com may be the biggest trigger. Let me tell you a story. My co-author of my book, Online Influence, he's been hired by Jorgen Schoen. He's been hired by booking for the last two years and they have an amazing test system. And they got these comments, remove all this social proof because I don't actually book because of that. I find it annoying. And in booking, they can just shut it off and the system knows when to shut it back on. They immediately, they drop the amount of bookings with 30 to 35%. That's millions and millions a day in booking.com. So does this work? Yes, of course it does work. It's deeply rooted in our systems. So let me share you an interesting research why. In the 60s, you had a scientist called Dr. Ash and he did an experiment where there were six people in the room and five were participants, five collaborated with him. Five participants were actually collaborating with Dr. Ash. So the sixth person was tested. And this is very famous experiment. So you might hear of it. So this is not the end explanation. It's only the introduction. And what they had to do was, oh, sorry. Let me open a new one. Okay, let's go. So the assignment was like this. There was one line and then the participants saw three lines next to it. And there was a simple question, which line is equally long as this line? Well, you can clearly see what was the right answer. In my example, it was this. But how was this set up? Set up was in the beginning, those five people answered the right answer. So they said, yes, it's line one is the same length as the original line. And then after some time, they start giving the wrong answer. So they say, this line is equally long. So five times it was people were presented or here at the wrong answer. The question was, was that what does the subject do the test subject, the six person in a far majority of the cases, the six person went against his own side. He clearly saw it's the wrong answer. But he said, this is the right answer. So we went with the crowd. This is a clear example of social proof and also the power because you go against every fiber in your body, you clearly see it's the wrong answer. And still they gave it. What's now the interesting thing about this? This research has been repeated with neuroscience brain scanning devices. And now they found something interesting. So there was the same setup as in the 60s. Now people were brain scanning devices. So at one moment, again, five people gave the wrong answer. As they say, this line is equally long. Two things lit up, two areas in the brain lift lit up with the six person. The first thing was puzzlement. What's going on? The second thing was pain, the area of the brain that's associated with pain. So this research shows that it causes us pain. Human beings, it causes pain to go against the group. Therefore social proof is and will remain such a powerful driver. Because the coolest thing about this experiment actually, they repeated it again. But now not with five human beings, but with five computers. When the computer start giving the wrong answer, there was still puzzlement. The area of puzzlement lit up, but no pain. And therefore people were willing to go against the computer. Fun thing to think about, how would that proceed with AI? Because we perceive it more and more that we like the computer as human beings. We interpreted what they say. But that's for the future. A great thing, and I like this to remember. And also it's a fascinating story, but for audiences, this explains why social proof is such a powerful influence principle. It's literally associated, it's research proven associated with pain in our system. It causes us pain to go against the group. So I prepared that one, I think that's a fascinating research, which helps you also to show the power of these principles. Nobody wants to have pain. And the second thing I want to, I see more and more hands are already up, so please continue. If some question pops up, I will answer them in a second. Put them down, raise your hand, love that. Love, I'm here to help. The second thing I prepared, and you know this story because it's in the practitioner course, is the story of Gordon Sinclair's restaurant. And Gordon Sinclair, a very successful restaurant in Chicago. I will not repeat the whole story, but just for people who do not have it top of mind, set the stage a bit. One problem is no shows. And no shows were people that booked a table in this restaurant, but didn't cancel, but just didn't show. There was a no show for him. But of course it was a problem because if people would contact the restaurant to say, I cannot show, we could book that table for another group. Gordon, as a sleut of influence, went out, went out as a sleut inside his organization and he found that when his staff took the reservation, they said something like this, when you want to come, well, Saturday, how many people, four people, great, we still have availability. Fantastic, I will book you. Please let us know when you're not coming. And then they hang up the phone. 30% no show. Now Gordon saw that and he changed only a few words, as I think the most of you will remember from that story. But he said, okay, do it like this. When you want to come, Saturday, how many people, four people, we have availability. Fantastic, I will book you the table. And now here comes the change. Not please contact us when you need to cancel. Will you please contact us if you need to cancel, if you cannot come. Will you, that was the change he made to the request. Will you please instead of the directive language, please call us. No shows dropped from 30% to 10%. A nice add on as I think some of you also know, I previously owned a company in the Netherlands where we sold kitchens. That's what people have in their houses. But Saturday was prime time, it's weekend, people have time and we face the same problem, but I discovered one thing more. On Saturday, it's meeting after meeting after meeting for all the sales reps and we had 20 shops and about at that day we had 70 sales reps, pool with meetings. But what I discovered, if the first meeting was a no show, the performance on the second meeting that showed was less because there was irritation, annoyance in the system of the sales rep. That was my conclusion. But I could not take another conclusion because if that not happened, the average performance was higher. I did exactly what Gordon Sinclair did and I was able, we had approximately 30% no show and I dropped it. I remember that percent to 15.7%, but it not only sold more kitchens on death specific meetings, which were rebooped. It also sold more kitchens on the second meeting because there was no frustration in any sales rep system. Why I'm sharing this, what's my nuance and deep dive takeaway that I want to give to all of you is this. There are three types of yeses. The first yes is an active yes. As you know, the activator of consistency, the principle of decision is getting a commitment. One of these amplifiers is an active commitment. I call this an active yes. The second one is a confirmation yes. Confirmation yes is yes, yes, I hear you. Oh yes, please call us. Yeah, yeah, yeah, I'm right. A lot of sales people think this is a great technique. Then you just say a word, I'm right. And people say, yeah, yeah, oh now I got a yes. That's a confirmation yes. Active yes is yes, I do this. Confirmation yes is I hear you. Yes, I hear you. The third one and that's a counter fight. Yes, counter fight. Yes, a counter fight. Yes, he sounds like this. Yes, I'm done with you. I don't want to listen anymore. Well, we clearly recognize the counter fight. Yes, where we make a lot of mistakes in recognizing what the other person gives us for signals is confusing and active. And a confirmation yes. How many of you ever had a conversation? Yeah, but you told me you were going to do that. Yeah, but you said to me you would buy that product for me. In their mind, in your client's mind, in the person you're communicating, they never said that. They said they were hearing you. So my new answer from now on, if you are working with the principle of commitment and consistency, you need to ask the question. And that's the magic that Gordon Sinclair instructed. He not only instructed two words, he said, will you please call us and then he instructed the staff to pause. And now the person replies, yes, I do this. This is an active yes. And if you just say, really, please call us. Yeah, yeah, yeah, you get these kind of yeses. Oh, isn't this a great product, right? Yeah, yeah. You don't get an active, then you get this one. We don't want confirmation yeses. Be aware of is this person actively committing to it. That's the one we want to have. And again, this is so very subtle in communication, because you hear the same word, you hear yes. But it's definitely not the same outcome that you will get. So to give you some extra deep dives, and maybe also fun for already for who signed up and asked for expectations on what we can expect in the mastermind. It's the principle of reciprocity which leads to cultivating relationships. So, at one moment you did this for one party and they will return. So, that was the picture I envisioned. And now again we come and I don't want to sound like a broken record. New answers and specific situations matter. So what we get here and I like this question a lot. Maybe these intangible gifts in a marriage are not so powerful and let me tell you. A bit why. It can be it can be. I recently read a book. But first let me explain this gifts versus and title months. Maybe you did this all your marriage and it's not perceived anymore as a gift. It said it perceived as an entitlement. Yes, of course, people are like, I'm not sure what you're talking about. It's perceived as an entitlement. Yes, of course, Pila takes care for the children. Of course, Pila cleans the house because he does that for the last 10, 20 years. So I don't perceive it as a gift. I perceive it as an entitlement. Now we have even a bigger problem if people perceive what we do for them as entitlements. Because if you stop doing it, they will be very angry. But you don't get anything in return. So in this little influence, we need to be sure that we don't provide things that people start to see as entitlements. And that's why I love the question that happens, especially I think in marriages and long term marriages. It happens a lot. You take things, start taking things for granted. Because I strongly believe the first time you start living together, he was super sweet and oh, thank you for cleaning the house and bit by bit that disappeared.
